description: This figure presents a comprehensive mechanical property assessment of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels, specifically illustrating the effect of manganese content (ranging from 24 to 34 wt%) on tensile strength, ductility, and impact toughness. The data comprises three vertically stacked subplots sharing a common x-axis (Mn content in wt%): the upper subplot displays ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) in MPa, showing both strengths peak around 26-30 wt% Mn (~1200 MPa for UTS and ~1000 MPa for YS) before declining sharply beyond 32 wt% Mn; the middle subplot tracks ductility parameters including reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles) in percent, revealing a plateau region between 28-32 wt% Mn followed by catastrophic deterioration at 34 wt% Mn; and the lower subplot shows U-notch impact toughness (Kcu, open diamonds) in J/cm², maintaining relatively stable values (~100-115 J/cm²) from 24-32 wt% Mn before plummeting to approximately 35 J/cm² at 34 wt% Mn. The caption specifies the alloy composition as Fe-9Al-0.9C with varying Mn, subjected to water quenching from 1050°C and aging at 550°C for 16 hours. The citation context explains that Mn increases the solubility of Al and C in the γ-solid solution, thereby reducing κ-phase precipitation during aging. The figure demonstrates that optimal structural properties—balancing high strength (UTS ≥ 1100 MPa) with acceptable toughness (>50 J/cm²)—are achieved within a narrow Mn window of approximately 28.5-30.5 wt%, with embrittlement occurring at higher Mn concentrations due to microstructural degradation.
